san-jose-ca.nativePlantPalettePara: San Jose native plant recommendations: native and adapted species for San Jose's climate reduce water consumption and maintenance costs compared to non-native alternatives. Willow Glen and Almaden Valley gardens blend native plants with structured design elements for curb appeal.
san-jose-ca.soilAmendmentPara: San Jose soil conditions: Bay Mud and alluvial deposits with expansive clay in Evergreen and Almaden Valley. Expansive clay requires compost amendment (3-4 inches tilled into the top 8 inches) to improve drainage and reduce compaction. Annual topdressing with compost maintains soil structure.
san-jose-ca.irrigationRegsPara: San Jose irrigation regulations: San Jose water conservation ordinances restrict irrigation schedules and may limit turf area in new installations; check with the local water authority for current watering-day restrictions.  Drip irrigation saves 30-50% over spray systems.
san-jose-ca.hardscapeStylePara: San Jose hardscape preferences: Willow Glen trends toward contemporary concrete pavers with clean lines, while Cambrian Park and Evergreen favor decomposed granite pathways with drought-tolerant plantings. Hardscape typically represents 20-30% of a comprehensive landscape project budget.
san-jose-ca.lawnMaterialPara: Lawn options in San Jose: traditional turf lawns are increasingly replaced by drought-tolerant grass varieties and reduced-area turf with native groundcovers.
san-jose-ca.turfAndXeriscapeRebatesPara: San Jose utility turf-removal rebates: check with the local water authority for current programs offering $1-3 per square foot for converting turf to water-efficient landscaping. These rebates frequently cover 30-50% of the conversion cost.
san-jose-ca.invasivePestPara: San Jose invasive species and pest concerns: standard regional pests and invasive species require ongoing monitoring and management. Integrated pest management (IPM) approaches reduce chemical dependency while maintaining landscape health.
san-jose-ca.HOAAestheticPara: Moderate HOA presence in parts of San Jose may restrict front-yard landscaping choices; verify CC&Rs before committing to a design plan.
san-jose-ca.treeServicePara: Tree work in San Jose: mature trees on Willow Glen and Almaden Valley properties may need pruning ($300-$1,500 per tree), removal ($500-$3,000 per tree), or hazard assessment ($100-$300) as they age. San Jose does not typically require permits for tree removal on private property, but heritage tree ordinances protect certain species. ISA-certified arborists ($150-$300 for consultation) provide independent assessment versus tree-service companies who profit from removal.
san-jose-ca.seasonalMaintenancePara: San Jose landscape maintenance calendar: spring and fall are the primary planting and renovation seasons; summer maintenance focuses on irrigation and mowing. Willow Glen and Almaden Valley professional maintenance contracts run $150-$400 per month.
san-jose-ca.stormwaterPara: San Jose stormwater management through landscaping: proper drainage is essential on San Jose's clay soil to prevent foundation damage; landscape grading should direct water away from structures.
san-jose-ca.commonScamPara: San Jose landscaping red flags: "landscape design included free" offers that produce generic plans without site-specific soil testing, sun-exposure analysis, or drainage assessment. Always verify CSLB license and ask for 3 references with photos of projects completed 2+ years ago to verify plant survival and design longevity.
